video_title: Ben Carlson: The Most Dangerous Investing Myth We All Love
gist: Ben Carlson (Ritholtz Wealth) and Meb Faber discuss market history through the lens of Carlson's book 'Risk and Reward,' arguing time horizon is the single most important variable in any investment, that even comically bad timing (Bob the worst market timer) still compounds positively over decades, and that today's biggest behavioral risk is the abundance of strategies/products tempting investors to tinker. They debate whether AI capex is a bubble (Carlson keeps an open mind), why the 1970s and Japan 1989 are the most instructive crises, and how tax management has become the new 'alpha' for wealthy clients.

themes.theme: Time horizon as the master variable

insights.claim: Defining your holding period before investing is the single most important decision for any asset, because returns are only knowable over the time horizon you actually commit to.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Investors should match instrument to horizon up front rather than reacting to interim drawdowns; mismatched horizons are the root cause of selling at the bottom.
insights.quote: defining your time horizon is everything for any investment. Doesn't care what asset you're in. You have to define how long you're planning on holding it for.

insights.claim: A hypothetical investor ('Bob') who only deployed cash at the four worst market peaks of the last 50+ years (1973-74, 1987, dot-com top, GFC) still finished a millionaire with roughly an 8% IRR because he never sold.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that entry timing matters far less than holding behavior and horizon; argues against waiting for the 'right' moment to invest.
insights.quote: he ended still up a millionaire cuz he just kept his money in stocks all time... I think the IRR was still like 8% per year or something.

insights.claim: Dollar-cost averaging consistently into the market beats holding cash and waiting to buy at the bottom of bear markets, because severe bear-market bottoms occur too infrequently to reward the cash drag.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Reinforces a 'stay invested' allocation default over market-timing cash strategies for long-horizon capital.
insights.quote: you're still better off at actually dollar cost averaging than like sitting on cash and waiting to buy at the bottom of a bear market. Cuz those huge bear markets don't happen very often.

insights.claim: Faber's framework for nervous new investors is to give three answers: the mathematically correct one (invest all now), the personally correct one (spread in over a year to diversify outcomes), and reconcile the gap with behavior.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A practical advisor template: lump-sum optimizes return but staged entry optimizes adherence, reducing the chance the client abandons the plan.
insights.quote: Correct answer mathematically is invest it all now... Now the correct answer for you personally is probably diversify the outcomes and just maybe go in over the course of a year.

insights.claim: Private credit funds carry a 7-to-10-year contractual term, so advisors who rushed clients in for the yield and then rushed out at the first sign of stress failed by ignoring the asset's required holding period.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Flags a behavioral/structural risk in the private credit boom: liquidity-mismatched investors will be forced sellers, a watch item for the asset class.
insights.quote: all the advisors who rushed in to put their clients in it because they had these high yields and then rushed out the first sign of pain was they didn't define their time horizon. Like this is a 7 to 10 year holding period.

themes.theme: Volatility compresses over time; short-term odds are near a coin flip

insights.claim: Over rolling 10-year periods stocks and bonds have roughly the same volatility, and at 30 years stock volatility is even lower, even though stocks are about four times as volatile as bonds over one year.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Justifies higher equity allocations for long-horizon portfolios since the perceived risk gap versus bonds largely disappears past a decade.
insights.quote: in the one year... Stocks are four times as volatile as bonds... but really, it starts to dampen at the 10-year rolling returns. Where stocks and bonds have roughly the same volatility.

insights.claim: The stock market has the edge for long-term holders (unlike a casino where the house edge grows with time), but on a daily basis only ~53-54% of trading days are positive, closer to a coin toss than investors assume.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Reframes equities as a positive-expectancy game only over long horizons; discourages short-term trading where the edge is minimal.
insights.quote: it's like 53% or 54% of trading days are positive... So it's closer to a coin toss on a daily basis than you would think.

insights.claim: Over the last 100 years the S&P 500 has had more years gaining 20%+ than it has had down years, making a 20%+ up year more likely historically than any down year.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Counters loss-aversion bias; the base rate strongly favors large gains, supporting staying invested through volatility.
insights.quote: there have been more 20% gains in the stock market in the S&P 500 than down years. So, you've been more likely historically to have a up year of 20% or more than a down year.

insights.claim: The 'average' 8-10% annual stock return almost never actually occurs; returns have landed in the 8-12% range only three or four times in history, with most years far higher or far lower.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations: investors should plan for wide annual dispersion, not the smooth average, and not panic at outsized down years.
insights.quote: that sort of 8, 9, 10% whatever the long-term average is, it basically never happens. It's happened like three or four times historically where the number has even been in like the 8 to 12% range.

insights.claim: The average up year is roughly +21% and the average down year roughly -15%, so short-term variability dominates the experience of investing despite a benign long-run average.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Explains why the realized path feels extreme; useful for setting client expectations and stress-testing risk tolerance.
insights.quote: The average up year, if I remember, is like 21% or something... and I think the average down year is like minus 15 or something.

themes.theme: Winning over time vs winning all the time

insights.claim: A Virtus/Morningstar study found that even top-quartile-over-a-decade funds spend only about two-thirds of rolling one-year periods in the top half, and are in the top quartile only ~53% of rolling five-year periods.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Even the best managers underperform frequently; investors who fire managers during inevitable cold streaks destroy the very outperformance they sought.
insights.quote: the top quartile fund that's in the top over 10 years on a rolling five-year basis, you're only in the top quartile half the time, 53%, which is astonishing.

insights.claim: The key to long-run outperformance is simply staying in the top half and avoiding extended time in the bottom quartile, because 'winning over time doesn't mean winning all the time.'
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Redefines manager/strategy selection around consistency and downside avoidance rather than chasing periodic top-quartile rankings.
insights.quote: winning over time doesn't mean winning all the time

insights.claim: A one-in-three chance of being in the bottom half in any given year mathematically guarantees occasional streaks of two or three consecutive bad years even for strong funds.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Investors should pre-commit to enduring multi-year underperformance streaks rather than treating them as evidence a strategy is broken.
insights.quote: one year in three, you're going to be in the bottom half... one in three means you get easily have one, two, three years in a row.

insights.claim: Even the best investing factors deliver only roughly a 60-70% win rate over three-to-five-to-ten-year windows, which is excellent but still implies frequent stretches of underperformance.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Calibrates expectations for factor strategies: any win rate above 50% is valuable, so patience through losing periods is the price of the premium.
insights.quote: If you can get like, I don't know, 60, 70% win rate over like three, five, 10 years, like you'll probably take that. That's pretty good.

themes.theme: Allocation dispersion is huge and timing the asset matters less than staying invested

insights.claim: The spread between the best and worst basic allocation strategies (60/40, endowment, risk parity, global market portfolio) averages about 20 percentage points per year, a far wider gap than most investors expect.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Allocation choice, not just security selection, drives enormous outcome dispersion, but the leaders rotate, arguing for diversification across allocations.
insights.quote: the spread between best and worst average like 20 percentage points a year.

insights.claim: An investor with perfect foresight picking the best of stocks vs bonds every year for 100 years would have earned only about 20% annually, while an investor who picked the worst asset every single year still made money.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Shows the bar for adding value via timing is absurdly high and that simply being invested in either asset is positive-expectancy; punishes attempts to lose.
insights.quote: if you flipped it, and you picked the exact wrong asset every year... you don't lose money. Which is crazy to me is like you get it wrong every single year, you pick the worst choice, and you still make money.

insights.claim: Despite a structurally rising-market backdrop, investors 'find a way' to lose money, performing worse than both the perfect-foresight and the worst-foresight mechanical strategies.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Behavior, not strategy, is the dominant destroyer of returns; argues for rules-based automation over discretionary trading.
insights.quote: and yet people find a way. Yeah, they find a way to lose money. It's even worse than the crystal ball.

themes.theme: 1970s inflation and the Japan bubble as the key historical lessons

insights.claim: The 1970s is the only decade in the last 100 years where cash (T-bills), bonds (10-year), and stocks all underperformed inflation, with inflation averaging 8% for the decade and never coming in below 3%.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Defines the worst-case regime for a traditional portfolio; in such a regime real assets (housing, gold, commodities) and short-term T-bills were the only refuges.
insights.quote: It was the only decade I could find on record... where cash, which is T-bills, bonds... and then stocks all underperformed inflation.

insights.claim: Stock valuation multiples come under serious pressure once inflation exceeds roughly 5%, and rising/volatile inflation hurts equities more than high-but-stable inflation.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Investors should watch the rate of change of inflation, not just the level; a stable 3% is tolerable for multiples while a fast move from 0% to 9% is not.
insights.quote: it's not only high inflation, but it's rising inflation from one year to the next where stocks tend underperform.

insights.claim: Carlson invokes Buffett's late-1970s argument that because equity ROE behaves like a perpetual bond, inflation mechanically erodes its real value in the short run, explaining why markets dislike rapidly rising inflation.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Provides a valuation mechanism (equities as inflation-sensitive perpetual bonds) for de-rating risk in inflationary regimes.
insights.quote: if you think about the ROE on stocks as like a perpetual bond, it actually makes sense that in the short run, inflation would eat into that.

insights.claim: Japan's 1989 bubble is the largest in history, with a CAPE ratio near 100 versus about 45 at the dot-com peak, and Tokyo's Imperial Palace grounds were reputedly worth more than all Canadian real estate.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Benchmarks how extreme valuations can get; useful reference point for judging today's concentration and any AI-driven excess.
insights.quote: it was 100 essentially for Japan in 1989 versus 45-ish or something for the dot-com bubble.

insights.claim: Japan grew from 15% of world equity market cap in 1979 to 45% by 1989 (larger than the US), then collapsed to about 5%, yet an investor in the All Country World Index from 1989 still earned roughly 8% annually through end-2025.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Global diversification absorbed even history's worst single-market collapse; argues against extreme home-country concentration.
insights.quote: even if you include Japan in that index and you invested in the ACWI... in 1989, over the next all the way through the end of 2025... you did 8% per year or something.

insights.claim: Bubbles recur because each generation has 'no natural immunity'; ~17 years after a crash a new cohort with no memory of pain takes over and repeats the cycle.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A generational lens for timing sentiment extremes; absence of crash memory among current participants is itself a risk signal.
insights.quote: How do these stupid bubbles happen over and over again? Because there's no natural immunity. Then you have an entire generation 17 years later that have never really experienced pain.

themes.theme: AI capex bubble debate and current cycle positioning

insights.claim: AI checks every box of prior manias (railroads, dot-com): a capex binge, market concentration, a single theme carrying the index, and technological innovation, yet Carlson deliberately refuses to declare it a bubble.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A measured 'strong opinions loosely held' stance; the obviousness of the bubble narrative itself argues for caution about the consensus call.
insights.quote: It's a capex binge and it's concentration and it's kind of the thing carrying the market and technological innovation. Like all the boxes are checked.

insights.claim: Carlson notes Anthropic is one of the fastest-growing companies in the world with revenue growth that 'boggles the mind,' even though profits are not yet there for many AI companies.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: The fundamental growth case complicates a pure bubble thesis; revenue trajectory may justify valuations that look extreme on profits.
insights.quote: Anthropic is one of the fastest-growing companies in the world. Like their revenue growth is just It kind of boggles the mind to think about how much money they're already making.

insights.claim: Faber argues a wave of mega IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic and one other) could match, even inflation-adjusted, the magnitude of all 1990s IPOs combined, raising the classic supply-glut risk that ends bull markets.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: A massive new-issuance supply shock is a historically reliable bull-market-ending mechanism worth monitoring as these deals price.
insights.quote: if all three of these go public, it's the equivalent in magnitude, even inflation-adjusted of all the '90s IPOs combined.

insights.claim: Anthropic was reported to be filing its S-1 the day of the recording, signaling a reopening of an IPO market that had seen little activity.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term catalyst; a reopening IPO window often coincides with late-cycle exuberance.
insights.quote: Anthropic said that they're going to file their S-1 today

insights.claim: The US has not experienced a real recession in nearly two decades (COVID being a money-flooded artificial pause), so it is unknown how a population already at record-low consumer sentiment would react to a genuine recession.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Untested behavioral fragility is a latent risk; a real recession could trigger an outsized sentiment and market reaction.
insights.quote: we haven't had a real recession in close to two decades... How are people going to react to an actual recession? It's like people are in a bad mood now when we don't have a recession.

insights.claim: Carlson cites Templeton's 'this time is different' warning but adds Templeton's lesser-known caveat that about 20% of the time things really are different, with the hard part being knowing which case you're in.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A humility framework for the AI debate: reflexively dismissing 'this time is different' is wrong ~20% of the time, arguing against dogmatic bubble calls.
insights.quote: he said, "Actually, 20% of the time things really are different."

insights.claim: Faber notes US equities are roughly two-thirds of world market cap today and frames a scenario where they could fall to one-third, with the cause only obvious in hindsight.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Extreme US dominance is itself a mean-reversion risk; the magnitude of a potential de-rating supports global diversification.
insights.quote: it's obvious why the US is no longer 2/3 of world market cap, it's only a third.

themes.theme: Tax management as the new alpha

insights.claim: Tax optimization (351 exchanges, long-short overlays, direct indexing) has become the primary place wealthy clients now seek 'alpha,' a major structural change in wealth management in the 2020s.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Identifies a durable growth area and product trend; controllable after-tax return is replacing pre-tax outperformance as the value-add advisors sell.
insights.quote: that's where people are trying to find alpha these days... I know that the tax side of things, I can kind of control that better... that's one of the biggest changes I've seen in the 2020s.

insights.claim: The most common problem among wealth-management clients today is oversized capital gains they want to diversify out of without triggering taxes, which Faber and Carlson reframe as a 'bull market problem' signaling they already won.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Signals how concentrated and appreciated client portfolios have become after a long bull market, driving demand for tax-deferral structures.
insights.quote: the biggest problem people have these days for wealth as far as wealth management clients go is, "Hey, my capital gains are too big. Help me diversify out of them without triggering too many gains."

insights.claim: Faber half-jokingly argues a 'nice fat bear market' is the best tax cleanup tool because it wipes out embedded capital gains.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Highlights that drawdowns create tax-loss-harvesting and reset opportunities, a silver lining for taxable investors.
insights.quote: nobody's mentioned is the best way to clean out these taxes... A nice fat bear market.

themes.theme: Behavior, life-cycle risk, and rules-based investing

insights.claim: Risk depends on life-cycle stage: a 20% loss on a mature $1M portfolio (-$200k) hurts far more than a 50% crash early in a career, so sequence-of-return risk dominates for retirees and near-retirees.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Asset allocation and de-risking should be driven by dollar exposure and remaining human capital, not by percentage volatility alone.
insights.quote: a 20% loss in dollar terms... you have a million dollar portfolio and you lose 20%, you're down $200,000... that stings more than a 50% crash early in your career when you have $10,000 at stake.

insights.claim: For young investors a bear market is not a risk but an opportunity to buy at lower prices; Carlson kept funding his 401k/Roth through 2008 reasoning those were the lowest prices he'd ever see and 'if the system implodes we're all screwed anyway.'
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Argues young accumulators should aggressively buy into crashes rather than de-risk, the opposite of the instinct that drives panic selling.
insights.quote: These are probably the lowest stock prices I'll ever see. And if it's if the financial system does implode, we're all screwed anyway. So, I might as well keep shoveling money in there.

insights.claim: It has never been harder to be patient because the proliferation of cheap, tax-efficient ETF strategies constantly tempts investors to tinker and reallocate, eroding discipline.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: The very democratization of strategies is now a behavioral liability; product abundance, not access, is the modern enemy of returns.
insights.quote: the fact that investors have so many more options makes it way harder than ever to just stick with something and not try to tinker and change.

insights.claim: Counterintuitively, people who saved and invested for decades often cannot spend down their portfolios in retirement because the wealth-building 'muscle' is the opposite of the spending muscle, leading them to live only off dividends.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A real client behavior gap that creates product demand for income/annuity structures that 'permission' spending.
insights.quote: people who have saved, invested for 20, 30, 40 years, they build those habits up... What's the thing they don't want to do? They don't want to see that number reduced.

insights.claim: Studies show annuity holders spend more in retirement because the income stream feels like a paycheck, even though much of the early payment is just the holder's own capital returned.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Behavioral framing of income products can solve the under-spending problem; supports product innovation in 'forced spending' structures.
insights.quote: the studies have actually shown that people with annuities spend it more cuz it feels like an income stream.

insights.claim: Carlson follows rules-based strategies because a rule 'doesn't quit to spend time with its family,' won't go through a divorce, and won't return capital to investors, removing the discretionary tail risks that derail managers.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Frames the case for systematic over discretionary investing as eliminating both the grand-slam and the strikeout, prioritizing consistency.
insights.quote: a rules-based strategy doesn't ever quit to spend time with its family... is not going to go through a divorce and decide to send money back to the investors.

insights.claim: Defining what you will NOT invest in is more important than defining what you will, with Carlson refusing leveraged/discretionary products and citing Jack White's 'liberation in limitations.'
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: An exclusion-first portfolio construction principle; a pre-set 'do not buy' list immunizes against being seduced by 3x and exotic products.
insights.quote: Talking about defining what you won't invest in is more important than what you will.

themes.theme: Active trading is a money incinerator

insights.claim: Hyperactive traders have an astonishingly bad batting average across forex, Brazilian futures, day trading and now prediction markets, all of which are 'absolute money incinerators.'
insights.speaker: Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Reinforces avoiding short-term speculative venues; the failure rate is consistent across instruments and eras.
insights.quote: It's like 90 We just see this with prediction markets today... forex of my childhood, Brazilian futures, day trading. They're all just absolute money incinerators.

insights.claim: In prediction markets roughly the top 0.1% of participants capture about 90% of the profits, much of it likely from insider information, making it nearly impossible for ordinary traders to win.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the extreme winner concentration in prediction markets; a strong deterrent for retail participation.
insights.quote: it's not even the top 1% but like the top 0.1% in these prediction markets that make like 90% of the profits or something and most of that is probably in some sort of insider trading.

insights.claim: The most dangerous outcome for a new trader is getting a few early wins, which breeds overconfidence and the belief that gains can be compounded indefinitely.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Early luck is a trap; advisors and investors should treat initial trading success as a warning rather than validation.
insights.quote: the worst thing that could happen is that you go into it and you get a few right and you go, "Oh, this is easy."

themes.theme: Bear market mechanics and secular cycles

insights.claim: Carlson identifies roughly six secular bull/bear markets over the last 100 years, each lasting 10-20 years, but the difficulty is that you cannot tell in real time whether a given decline is cyclical within a secular bull or the start of a secular bear.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A regime framework for context, paired with the humility that regime identification is only clear in hindsight.
insights.quote: I figured there's been like six of them... they're all like 10 to 20-year periods of like really crappy returns and really great returns... it's hard to know when you're in the midst of like a cyclical one.

insights.claim: Bear-market bottoms are impossible to identify in real time because the price action coming out of a true bottom looks identical to a dead-cat bounce, so no one believes the recovery at first.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Argues against trying to call the bottom; the indistinguishability of real recoveries from fake rallies favors staying invested.
insights.quote: you get the same action coming out of a dead cat bounce as you do coming out of a bear market. Like, stocks fly... And then they potentially fall back.

insights.claim: The current bull market spanning ~16-17 years has included a 35% COVID crash, a 25% bear in 2022, and a couple of ~19% drawdowns, yet is still classified as one broader secular bull.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Frames how much volatility a secular bull can contain; sharp drawdowns are not necessarily regime changes.
insights.quote: We had a crash in COVID, that was 35%. We had the bear market in 2022, that was you know, 25%... but it's still kind of a broader bull market.

insights.claim: The 2022 decline was a roughly 25% non-recessionary bear market that recovered to new highs within two years, which Carlson characterizes as 'run-of-the-mill' versus a long, grinding recessionary bear.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Distinguishes quick non-recessionary bears from prolonged recessionary ones; the US hasn't endured the latter since 2008.
insights.quote: 2022 was kind of like a run-of-the-mill, like, I call it a non-recessionary bear market... 25%? It lasted a year or so, new highs within 2 years.

insights.claim: Other major markets (e.g., China with ~30 years of no returns, and post-2009 US defying universal 'lower-than-average returns' forecasts) show outcomes are far more surprising than anyone can predict from current information.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: History's main lesson is humility about forecasts, not predictive power; supports diversification over conviction bets on any single market's trajectory.
insights.quote: who in their right mind coming out of 2009 would have said, "The US is about to embark on a 16-year bull market that is going to come close to rivaling the '80s and '90s."
